title: Oklahoma's Latest "Abortion Ban" (SB612) Isn't Worth the Paper It's Printed On
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman dissects Oklahoma SB612, arguing the headline-grabbing 'abortion ban' will not stop a single abortion because it lacks language defying the federal judiciary, undervalues preborn children with a 10-year cap, exempts hormonal birth control, and grants full immunity to mothers who self-manage abortions. He frames it as a recurring political theater that lets Republicans posture as pro-life while leaving abortion legal in practice.
audiences: pro-life voter trying to assess whether SB612 is a real win
Oklahoma legislator weighing abolition vs. incremental bills
pastor or church leader discipling members on civil government
journalist covering state-level abortion bans post-Roe

questions.question: Why does Silberman say SB612 is unenforceable despite passing the Oklahoma House?
questions.answer: Because the bill contains no clause directing the Attorney General or state agencies to enforce it in defiance of federal court rulings. Without that language, courts will simply enjoin the law and abortion will continue, exactly as happened with Alabama's Human Life Protection Act in 2019.
questions.quote: There are a number of problems with this bill, the first being that it contains no language clarifying that the prohibition contained in SB612 is to be enforced in the face of judicial violations of the right to life.

questions.question: What pending Oklahoma bill did contain the enforcement language SB612 lacks, and what did it require?
questions.audience: Oklahoma legislator weighing abolition vs. incremental bills
questions.answer: SB495, left pending in the Senate Health and Human Services Committee, would have required the Attorney General to monitor and direct enforcement of homicide statutes against abortion regardless of contrary federal authority.
questions.quote: The Attorney General shall monitor this state's enforcement of [Oklahoma homicide statutes] in relation to abortion. The Attorney General shall direct state agencies to enforce those laws regardless of any contrary or conflicting federal statute, regulation, treaty, executive order, or court ruling.

questions.question: What precedent does Silberman cite to predict SB612 will be enjoined?
questions.audience: journalist covering state-level abortion bans post-Roe
questions.answer: Alabama's 2019 Human Life Protection Act, where Governor Kay Ivey openly admitted at signing that the bill might be unenforceable and Planned Parenthood was confident enough to announce a new Birmingham mega-center within a month.
questions.quote: Planned Parenthood was so confident that the courts would not allow the law to stand they told the press they would be building a new mega-center in Birmingham a month after Ivey signed the Human Life Protection Act.

questions.question: How does Governor Stitt's posture mirror Governor Ivey's, according to the article?
questions.audience: pro-life voter trying to assess whether SB612 is a real win
questions.answer: Stitt has openly deferred to the courts on previous bills like HB1102, signaling in advance that he will let federal judges decide whether the law stands rather than enforcing it as the supreme law of Oklahoma.
questions.quote: We'll let the courts work out if any of those get overturned.

questions.question: What does Silberman call the recurring pattern of pro-life legislation in Oklahoma?
questions.answer: He calls it a 'game' in which Republican politicians appear to abolish abortion, the courts predictably enjoin the law, and politicians repeat the cycle the next session without ever protecting preborn children.
questions.quote: Stitt will sign it. Republican politicians will posture as if they just abolished abortion. The courts will enjoin enforcement of the law. Republican politicians will say "shucks," pretend they did all they could, and start the same process over again next year.

questions.question: Why does the 10-year maximum sentence in SB612 violate equal protection?
questions.audience: pastor or church leader discipling members on civil government
questions.answer: Capping punishment for killing a preborn child at ten years treats those children as less valuable than born people, which Silberman frames as both biblical partiality and a 14th Amendment violation requiring equal protection of the laws.
questions.quote: Providing only 10-year maximum sentence treats preborn children as less valuable than born people. This is partiality which God condemns in His Word, and it also violates the 14th amendment of the United States Constitution which establishes that "no state shall… deny to any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the law."

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue SB612 actually protects a major form of abortion?
questions.audience: pro-life voter trying to assess whether SB612 is a real win
questions.answer: Because the bill explicitly exempts birth control drugs administered before pregnancy, even though hormonal birth control can thin the uterine lining and prevent an already-conceived embryo from implanting, making it abortifacient in those cases.
questions.quote: These abortions are not recorded but are likely the most prolific form of abortion that take place and are explicitly protected by SB612.

questions.question: How does SB612 treat mothers who procure their own abortions?
questions.answer: The bill grants mothers full criminal immunity, allowing only the abortionist to be prosecuted, which Silberman says effectively preserves a mother's ability to murder her own child through self-managed abortion.
questions.quote: This section does not… authorize the charging or conviction of a woman with any criminal offense in the death of her own unborn child.

questions.question: What is Silberman's overall verdict on whether SB612 is worth celebrating?
questions.audience: journalist covering state-level abortion bans post-Roe
questions.answer: He flatly rejects the celebratory framing, arguing the bill will never take effect, contains structural injustices even if it did, and was passed in lieu of legislation that would actually have abolished abortion immediately.
questions.quote: SB612 is not a bill to celebrate. It will not go into effect because of its refusal to stand up to the federal judiciary. Even if it did go into effect, it has major problems that allow for abortion and frame injustice into law.

related.title: BREAKING: SB495 Might be Heard in Senate HHS Committee on Feb. 3!
related.reason: SB495 is the Oklahoma bill Silberman holds up as the contrast model that contained the AG enforcement language SB612 omits.

related.title: OK Legislature Sends 3 Useless Pro-Life Bills to Governor Stitt
related.reason: Covers HB1102 and the prior batch of bills Silberman references as proof of Stitt's 'let the courts decide' posture being repeated.

related.title: Free the States Memo to OK Senators Regarding SB1728
related.reason: Earlier critique of David Bullard's SB1728, the bill whose 'thousands of unborn babies will be saved' rhetoric Silberman quotes here.

related.title: Remember Bullard's Claim That "thousands of unborn babies will be saved every year" by SB1728
related.reason: Directly documents the Bullard 'thousands saved' / 'abortion desert' boast Silberman uses to expose Dahm's identical posturing on SB612.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the broader abolitionist framework explaining why bills like SB612 that defer to the federal judiciary are categorically rejected.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the judicial supremacy critique that underlies Silberman's central charge that SB612 surrenders to a 'genocidal federal judiciary.'

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Background on the Oklahoma political dynamic that produces self-congratulatory but unenforceable bills like SB612 year after year.
